posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
A great Wednesday night Burg tasting arranged by dcornutt. The theme was 2019, format single blind. Originally was going to be mostly village and 1er, but the usual suspects got involved and shifted things just a little bit. And with 21 bottles for 8 guys there was certainly enough to go around.

General concensus was 2019 is shaping up to be a solid vintage across the board. So much ripe fruit, but tons of acidity to balance it out. Everything was drinking on point, very enjoyable and nothing was closed down.

2006 Pol Roger - quick intro glass

Whites

AOC Flight
Coche - classic Coche nose but very little reduction. Precise and tailored.
Boisson - huge nose and lots of reduction. Very full and luscious. Half the room guessed these wrong. Certainly not a 10x quality delta to match the price difference.

Chablis Flight
Dauvissat La Forests
Dauvissat Les Clos
Les Clos was considerably better on all accounts, body, flavor, finish.

Meursault Flight
Bernard Bonin Limozin
Boisson Grands Charron
Roulot Clos des Boucheres
Bonin was the surprise as nobody has heard of him before, Boisson was corked and Roulot was Roulot.

Batard Flight
Gagnard Delagrange
Blain Gagnard
JM Boillot
There was much debate about the difference between the Gagnards as the concensus is that they are vinified by the same individual from different vines. That said, the Blaine Gagnard was clearly superior in depth and body. Boillot was good, but Blain wins this flight.

Monty Flight
Jadot
Sauzet
A late entry by a couple of very generous guests, both decanted. Sauzet was the clear favorite, well balanced, layer upon layer of flavor and a finish that just keeps going and going. Jadot was very good also, clearly the second best white of the evening.

Reds

Village Flight
Duroché Gevrey-Chambertin
Grivot Vosne Romanée
Duroché is just so crushable. Very primary and open. Grivot had loads of spice and herbs, will certainly be better over time.

1er Flight
Clavelier Aux Brulees
Confuron Les Beaux Monts
These were both very nice, Clavelier more open and took the edge. Confuron needs a lot more time to show.

RSV Flight
L’Arlot
Confuron
L’Arlot is everything you want in a graceful, sultry red. Just absolutely firing on all cylinders. I recently had the Suchots and this is very similar but with more of everything. Confuron was a bit tighter and less yielding at this juncture. Fun flight for my palate.

Wildcard GC Flight
Mongeard-Mugneret Richebourg
Bouchard Chambertin
MM Rich was clearly a Rich, so powerful, muscular and majestic. The opposite of the RSV flight. Bouchard was a stumper as nobody was thinking Gevrey. Such a different profile between the two.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked:
Well that looks like a blast!

I’ve had 19 Dauvissat forest and Preuses and thought the gap was pretty close, with the GC leading in purity and elegance… but he really knocked it out of the park this year (including AOC Chablis as has been mentioned elsewhere)

That’s a heck of a tasting, and I’d agree with your characterization (at least with regard to whites) that there is good intensity of fruit balanced by ample acids.

posts.name: Ch@rlie F|_|
posts.cooked:
Bonin was the surprise as nobody has heard of him before, Boisson was corked and Roulot was Roulot.

Bonin is the new hotness for “natural” crowd despite not really being natural. Biodynamic, no chaptalization, minimal sulfur etc. Good stuff.

posts.name: John Vizuete
posts.cooked: Also they are freaking delicious. 18 Meursault Genevrieres was gorgeous.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Bonin was the surprise as nobody has heard of him before, Boisson was corked and Roulot was Roulot.

Bonin is the new hotness for “natural” crowd despite not really being natural. Biodynamic, no chaptalization, minimal sulfur etc. Good stuff.

That’s weird, I hadn’t realized it was a “natural” wine. If anything, it drinks like a very clean traditional white Burgundy to me with minimal reduction; I quite like them. Had a 19 Limoizin in Paris last week that was really good. (To maintain my meagre natty bona fides I did have a Prieur Roch NSG VV as well with that dinner.)

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Andrew, what do you mean by “classic Coche nose but very little reduction”? I associate Coche with reduction (and love it), though there has certainly been a change in style there in recent years under R.

That was why there was such the debate. Half the people thought the more reduced nose was the Coche just because it was more reduced. But I think the Coche nose is more than just reduction and this vintage seems to confirm some changes at the Domaine.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: Nice notes Andrew, thanks. If Coche has gotten rid of the buttered popcorn, then I don’t feel bad about being priced out going forward. I have a couple of bottles of the Boisson Meursault waiting for me locally that I need to pick up. I am looking forward to trying that. Interesting note on the MM Rich. I was on the fence about buying this one because the GE is half the price and outstanding, but this might put me over the edge.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked: In all seriousness, Raphael has clearly been making the wines differently for almost decade now - it’s not currently being made in a reductive style.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: My '19’s have not arrived yet but will be interesting to see how reductive they show on release. The price has gone up $300/btl on the Meursault since I ordered a few months ago, just nuts. I am really hoping the Boisson shows well at 1/10th the price.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
In all seriousness, Raphael has clearly been making the wines differently for almost decade now - it’s not currently being made in a reductive style.

More than a decade, in fact.

The nutty/smoky quality of the Boisson wines is significantly related to the fact that they work with Tonnelerie Gillet. You will find some of the same aromatic signatures, applied to very different base wines, chez Niellon, another Gillet loyalist!

We had a hipster Somm at Roberta’s in Culver City decline a taste of our 06 Allemand, saying “I only drink natural wines.”

JY and I were up in Portland once and we went to a restaurant where the Somm was famous for her stance being very pro natural wine (roughly 5-6 years ago)

We brought 2011 allemand Reynard and 2011 allemand sans soufre. I was like - yo! This SS has no sulfur!

She would have none of it.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Sorry to be late to this thread. Andrew very nice concise evaluations of the wines. It was so much fun. Everyone was very generous and we had an amazing selection of wines from this fabulous 2019 vintage. I have a few favorites.

I was shocked by the beauty of the Boisson Bourgogne. Coche is no slouch and I thought on the nose the Boisson was the Coche but that was my mistake. Mainly reduction led me there. Tremendous wine anyway even if not quite as deep on the palate as the Coche.

Loved the Les Clos. The Bernard-Bonin was a first time try for me. To me it was double blind. It was the class of the entire flight even though the Roulot was a premier cru. Shockingly great.

I understand that Marc Antonin Blain makes the Gagnard-Delegrange wines so it is not a surprise that that wine was very similar to the Blain-Gagnard which was terrific.

The Sauzet Montrachet is a special wine. Perfect balance. Even while young it has everything. An incredible treat. Thanks to George Chang.

The reds were uniformly excellent. Floored by the power of the Mongeard-Mugneret Richebourg. Amazing texture too.

A wonderful night of exploration and fun.

Thanks sincerely to Marty Allen for the venue and organizing the fine details.

Now to find some Bernard-Bonin. (It doesn’t have that “natty” feel to me. This is the real deal. )

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Hi Andrew K - thanks for the TNs.

Did you find any of the 2019 red tasted above turned jammy after a few hours in the glass ?

I asked because in the thread : Which 2019 Burgundy Have You Loaded Up ON - you posted on 2022/03/30 that …long haul, this is brooding. This one needs some more time in the cellar to come together. After a couple of hours in the glass this turns almost jammy. Very ripe finish. (88 pts.) 2019 Paul Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet,

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Hi Andrew K - thanks for the TNs.

Did you find any of the 2019 red tasted above turned jammy after a few hours in the glass ?

I asked because in the thread : Which 2019 Burgundy Have You Loaded Up ON - you posted on 2022/03/30 that …long haul, this is brooding. This one needs some more time in the cellar to come together. After a couple of hours in the glass this turns almost jammy. Very ripe finish. (88 pts.) 2019 Paul Pillot Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet,

Peter. I am not Andrew but the Domaine Paul Pillot reds from Chassagne are routinely the ripest in every vintage. 2019 seems to me (IMHO) a vintage where you could easily overdo this. I own the Clos St Jean rouge from Thierry. It is easily the densest and ripest 2019 I own. FWIW

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Hi Andrew K - thanks for the TNs.

Did you find any of the 2019 red tasted above turned jammy after a few hours in the glass ?

We had quite a bit of left overs at the end of the night and I tasted the Richebourg again and some others. Can’t say anything about all the wines, but I didn’t find any I tasted had turned jammy. Also, I brought home the L’Arlot RSV and corked it with a Repour. I had the rest of the bottle last night and it was even better. So elegant and ethereal, bursting with primary fruit, perfectly balanced ripeness, Grand Cru density and yet lifted on the palate, long, long finish. It had opened up nicely with the extra air and was stunning. 98 pts.

posts.cooked:

So elegant and ethereal, bursting with primary fruit, perfectly balanced ripeness, Grand Cru density and yet lifted on the palate, long, long finish. It had opened up nicely with the extra air and was stunning. 98 pts.

Much appreciated your feed-back and wonderful news for me at least.

I skipped the red 2018 and concentrate on buying the red 2019 from my preferred producers; but most of them are from village level or Bourgogne. I have not open any of them yet ( to avoid travel-shock ). I will continue to load-up the 2019 red and most likely will avoid the 2020 red ( as I predict they will be more pricey and hard to get).

title: 2019 Burg Tasting: Full Monty of Infanticide
